one_line_takeaway: An honest-but-curious FL server that logs pre/post-unlearning model parameters can invert the parameter difference to reconstruct or re-identify exactly the data federated unlearning was meant to erase, so FU as currently designed can amplify rather than remove privacy risk.
key_finding: FUIA reconstructs forgotten data and identifies forgotten classes across all three FU scenarios and clearly beats the MUIA baseline. In sample- and client-unlearning image reconstruction, FUIA reaches best-case MSE around 0.002 and PSNR near 39 dB, versus MUIA reconstructions that stay around MSE 0.09-0.23 / PSNR ~20-22 dB. For class unlearning it identifies a single forgotten class with 100% predictive probability (Table I). Attack strength depends heavily on the FU type and setting: FUIA is stronger against approximate FU methods (UnrollingSGD, FUEraser) than against exact Retraining, degrades as client data volume grows (256 samples/client) or as more samples/classes are forgotten (4-class inference drops to 80% correct, 20% failure), and performs markedly better under FedSGD than FedAvg because FedSGD exposes exact rather than approximate gradients. Two defenses trade privacy for utility: gradient pruning at p>0.6 defeats reconstruction, and Gaussian gradient perturbation at std>0.003 removes most features.
core_contribution: The paper introduces FUIA (Federated Unlearning Inversion Attack), which the authors frame as the first systematic study of privacy leakage inherent to federated unlearning (FU) itself. The key insight is that the parameter difference between the original global model (W_o) and the unlearned global model (W_u) approximates the gradient of the forgotten data, so an attacker who has stored these parameters can invert them to recover the forgotten data the FU process was supposed to erase. FUIA is designed for all three FU scenarios: sample unlearning and client unlearning (where it reconstructs the image features of forgotten data via gradient inversion) and class unlearning (where it infers the label of the forgotten class from output-layer weight/bias changes). Note: FedU, FRAMU, FedEraser, FedRecovery, FURF, FUCDP, DLG/iDLG/STG, R-GAP, COPA, and MUIA are all cited prior work in Related Work/Preliminaries, not contributions of this paper; MUIA (Hu et al. 2024) in particular is used only as the comparison baseline, and the authors argue it does not transfer to FL because per-round parameter mixing across clients corrupts the gradient signal.
threat_model: The attacker is the FL server, modeled as honest-but-curious: it follows the prescribed training and unlearning protocols but passively records the per-round parameter updates uploaded by each client during both the FL training phase and the unlearning phase. It does not interfere with training, does not act maliciously, and is assumed to have no explicit knowledge of the specific FU mechanism, so it obtains only an approximation of the forgotten-data gradient rather than exact values. The authors argue this is a weak/realistic assumption because many FU methods already store historical updates to enable efficient unlearning. For class unlearning the server is additionally assumed to know the global label set (not individual client data). The attack goal is to reconstruct features (images) of forgotten samples/clients or to infer the label of a forgotten class after unlearning completes.
limitations: The attack is only an approximation under FedAvg (the realistic aggregation), which the authors repeatedly note weakens reconstruction relative to FedSGD. Effectiveness drops as client data volume increases (only 1 of 4 clients reconstructed clearly at 256 samples/client under exact Retraining) and as the number of forgotten samples/classes grows. Demonstrations use very small per-client sample counts (e.g., 8 or even 1 sample/client in headline experiments), limiting realism. Evaluation is confined to two CIFAR image datasets, two ResNet architectures, image-classification tasks, and FedAvg; only three example FU methods (one exact, two approximate) are tested. The class-unlearning attack recovers only labels, not features, and can misrank when forgotten and remaining classes are correlated. Both defenses impose real accuracy loss (baseline 84.10% falling to 72.27% at pruning p=0.6 and 76.43% at perturbation std=0.003).

quantitative_results: Best-case FUIA image reconstruction: MSE ~0.0020, PSNR ~38.98 dB (sample unlearning, approximate FU)
MUIA baseline reconstructions in the same figures stay around MSE 0.09-0.23 and PSNR ~20-22 dB, visibly inferior to FUIA
Class unlearning: 100% predictive probability of identifying a single forgotten class (Table I)
Multi-class unlearning: correct labels for 2 and 3 forgotten classes, but 4-class inference is fully correct only 80% of trials (20% failure)
Defense gradient pruning: p>0.6 destroys reconstruction; test accuracy 78.32% (p=0.5), 72.27% (p=0.6), 67.78% (p=0.7), 62.21% (p=0.8) vs 84.10% no-defense (Table II)
Defense gradient perturbation (Gaussian noise): std>0.003 removes most features; accuracy 80.64% (0.001), 76.43% (0.003), 72.33% (0.005), 65.32% (0.009) (Table II)
Client-unlearning auxiliary-term weight gamma=0.1 gave best reconstruction; MSE rose / PSNR fell for gamma>0.1 (Fig. 10)
FedSGD aggregation yields substantially better attack quality than FedAvg (e.g., MSE 0.0015 / PSNR 26.20 vs poorer FedAvg values, Fig. 13)
baselines_compared: MUIA (Machine Unlearning Inversion Attack, Hu et al. 2024) adapted to FL using the global-model difference as the forgotten-data gradient
Retraining (exact FU) vs approximate FU methods UnrollingSGD (sample), FUEraser (client), FUCDP (class)
FedAvg vs FedSGD aggregation as an attack-condition comparison
